data.title: The Chemist's Triple-Factor Closed-End Fund Report, October 2025
data.description:
The "Triple-Factor" Closed-End Fund Report

(This was formerly the "High-High-Low" CEF Report, now renamed to better reflect the systematic screening for three key attributes: high yield, strong coverage, and discount to NAV).

Our systematic approach screens over 400 closed-end funds to identify candidates meeting three critical criteria that balance yield, distribution sustainability, and value. This screening methodology originated from a member's suggestion:

Stanford, you should do a follow up article on CEF that distribute 8%+, have 90% or more coverage and trade under NAV. What does that look like in today's world? How many funds are doing that? What are the Z scores? I think many investors would be interested who are pushing for higher yield to identify best in class of aggressive funds that are coming close to covering dividends. Allows people to reevaluate risk/reward.

Therefore, the Triple-Factor screen identifies funds with the combination of:

  1. Substantial yield (>6.5%)
  2. Strong distribution coverage (>90%)
  3. Value opportunity (trading at a discount to NAV)

How is coverage calculated?

For the following fund houses, the latest trailing coverage numbers from the fund's latest unaudited monthly estimates are used:

  • PIMCO: trailing 6-month coverage
  • BlackRock: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Nuveen: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Eaton Vance: trailing 3-month coverage

Coverage ratios for the remaining funds are based on earnings data from their latest annual or semi-annual reports. Since these reports are published just twice yearly, these coverage figures may lag by up to 6 months compared to funds that provide monthly earnings estimates.

I hope that these rankings of Triple-Factor CEFs will provide fertile ground for further exploration and due diligence. As a reminder, these top lists are meant to be preliminary shortlists for further research, and are not meant to be the final analysis for a buy or sell decision.

Key to table headings:

P/D = premium/discount

Z = 1-year z-score

Lev = leverage

BE = baseline expense

Cov = coverage

Data were taken from the close of October 17, 2025.

data.title: Sandvik: Staying Neutral Until Revenue Growth Inflects
data.description:
Investment Action

I had a hold rating for Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY)(OTCPK:SDVKF) previously, as I thought the valuation was not cheap. While I saw a 40% upside over 4 years, that equates to only 10%/year, which I thought was not attractive. I was obviously wrong, as the share price went up ~15% in just ~3 months. The Q3 2025 results showed solid order momentum, notable recovery in MIM, and MRS saw good growth as it benefited from strong metals pricing. However, despite my higher conviction in management’s long-term guidance, valuation now looks full after the recent rally. At around 20x forward PE, near-term upside appears limited unless order strength quickly translates into revenue acceleration.

Q3 2025 Earnings Showed Strength

SDVKY 3Q25 results showed solid underlying demand, though reported figures were weighed down by FX effects and slower revenue conversion. Total orders grew 7% y/y to SEK 30.8 billion on a reported basis, but adjusted for FX, organic growth was 16%. Revenue came in at SEK 29.2 billion, down 4% as reported but up 5% organically, primarily due to timing delays between order intake and delivery. By segment, Mining and Rock Solutions [MRS] saw organic order intake up 24%. Even excluding the SEK 1.6 billion in major orders, organic growth was still 16%. Machining and Intelligent Manufacturing [MIM] saw 8% organic order growth, while Rock Processing [RP] saw 9% organic growth (or 14% excluding major orders).

While order growth was solid, profitability was weak, mainly due to external factors. SDVKY reported adj. EBITA of SEK 5.5 billion, down 6% y/y, with margins down 40 bps to 19%, and this was mostly due to SEK 837 million in FX headwinds that diluted margin by 130 bps. This resulted in an adj. profit of SEK 3.5 billion (EPS SEK 2.81, down 4% y/y).

Order Intake Signals Accelerating Growth

As you can see above, the clear highlight for SDVKY this quarter was the strength of its order intake. The 16% organic order growth significantly outpaced the 5% organic revenue growth by a full 1100 bps, which basically confirms that underlying demand remains solid. In fact, it has gotten better since this was an acceleration from 1H25. This order strength pushed the book-to-bill ratio to 105%, marking the third straight quarter above 100%, suggesting continued backlog growth, which gives better revenue visibility and meaningfully reduces near-term growth risk. This order momentum did not stop in Q3. Management commentary implied this order momentum has persisted into the Q4 quarter. Specifically, they commented that daily order intake for the first two weeks of October was stable relative to Q3 levels, after adjusting for normal seasonality. This is very encouraging because it suggests that underlying demand is not a one-off blip.

This sets up very well for FY2026, as orders received today would translate into revenue growth in the coming quarters. With momentum being strong in Q3 and even Q4, there is a good chance FY2026 will see revenue growth acceleration (remember organic growth in Q3 was only 5%).

MRS Segment

The core driver for SDVKY’s equity story is its MRS segment, as I mentioned before, and it is great to learn that it is showing accelerating momentum. The 24% y/y organic order growth was great, but even better if you consider that equipment orders grew 75% organically and that brownfield projects came in at ~70% of equipment orders vs. the usual 50%, which I believe reflects genuine pent-up demand from miners reinvesting after a period of under-investing.

The consequential impact from an accelerating equipment growth is that it extends the customer lifetime value as SDVKY benefits from more aftermarket sales opportunities. And as expected, the aftermarket business continues to be robust (still at low 70% of total MRS revenue) with double-digit organic order growth in Parts and Services.

You might ask what is really driving the sudden surge in equipment orders here. I see a few reasons for this. Firstly, crucial metals continue to fetch high prices, especially gold and copper, which were explicitly called out by management as very strong drivers in Q3 and were ~60% of the MRS business. High prices naturally incentivize miners to act. Secondly, brownfield is the natural choice for miners because it is faster to ramp it up vs. greenfield. And because of this “shorter cycle,” SDVKY is able to see a surge in equipment orders (for greenfield, because of the timeline, equipment orders come later). Thirdly, it links back to what I said before. There is an aging global mining fleet that needs replacement, and given the pricing environment, it makes sense for miners to commit and upgrade their equipment.

Very importantly, this accelerating equipment sales cycle heavily improves customer lifetime value [CLTV]. Remember that each new piece of machinery (particularly those featuring SDVKY’s AutoMine and digital automation platforms) makes the relationship stickier between SDVKY and the customer. Once deployed, these technologies become mission-critical for a mine’s daily operations and also generate performance data for analytics and tracking purposes. As such, these machines require ongoing software updates to make sure they are working properly to ensure full productivity. This dynamic makes miners highly reliant on SDVKY’s continuous “service” and software ecosystem to operate effectively, and all these significantly increase switching costs.

As you can imagine, every new sale then enhances the CLTV because of the higher-margin aftermarket activity. This dynamic is already visible. The aftermarket business maintained strong momentum in 3Q25, delivering double-digit organic order growth in parts and services.

Machining Recovery

Another very encouraging development in Q3 2025 was the recovery in MIM. After several quarters of poor demand that had dampened the stock sentiment, finally, MIM delivered 8% organic order growth, which marked an inflection point. Importantly, this rebound was broad-based rather than dependent on one-off tailwinds or pricing effects. The core part of this business (cutting tools) saw high-single-digit organic order growth y/y, and management noted that demand remained stable sequentially, with a strong finish in September. This gives confidence that the trough may have passed, and Q4 may continue to see this momentum. Aside from the cutting tool, the powder business also performed well, delivering low double-digit growth, which I expect to continue because I believe tungsten prices will stay elevated for the foreseeable future due to ongoing supply constraints.

On an overall basis, management did also mention that daily orders in early October were stable vs. Q3 2025 levels, which suggests that this recovery trend could be a real start of a growth inflection. The rebound in MIM is very important for the business because it diversifies SDVKY beyond just mining. As industrial production normalizes eventually, the MIM segment should see strong recovery. The reason is straightforward. The core products in this MIM segment are metal-cutting tools, and these are consumables used across multiple industries (ranging from automotive and aerospace to general engineering). When factory utilization increases, the machines run longer, and that naturally drives up consumption and replacement demand for these tools.

My sense is that we are inching towards an inflection here because management commented on stabilization in general engineering demand during Q3 2025. If this industrial cycle continues to move in the right direction, MIM should see revenue growth acceleration in the coming quarters.

Valuation

I am not changing my long estimates for SDVKY, as I still think management's long-term guidance is a good anchor to get a sense of the upside. My conviction of SDVKY meeting this target has certainly gone up, but given the share price increase so far, I am still not so sure if I want to give this a buy rating. Even after adjusting my multiple expectations to 20x forward PE on FY30 estimates (which is already a premium to peers that have a similar revenue growth outlook of high single digits on average), I get to 11% IRR (or 36% upside over 3 years). To be fair, 11% IRR is not bad for most retail investors, but I am seeking >15% IRR opportunities.

Hence, I am still going to stick to my hold rating. But for those that want to give this a shot. I see a scenario where near-term IRR could be higher than 11%. If SDVKY is able to show real inflection in revenue growth (translated from the current strong order book growth), the market may very well push multiples higher against the backdrop of faster earnings growth in the near term. In this case, we could see another round of >15% upside in just a few months (like it did recently).

Risk

The near-term risk is a significant FX headwind, which could lead to poorer than expected reported results. There are also internal execution risks. The Mining ERP transition execution has not been as great (residual costs from this impacted Q3 2025 margins by 30 bps), and should this not be fixed, we could see this linger into Q4 2025. Furthermore, converting the strong order book into timely revenue remains key. This conversion may not happen in Q4, and that could weigh on sentiment.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating on SDVKY. The order momentum across MRS and MIM is very solid, and its aftermarket mix makes the long-term growth outlook better. However, order conversion timing and valuation make me stay cautious. I would turn more bullish if stronger revenue translation and margin recovery emerge in the coming quarters. For now, I agree SDVKY is a solid long-term name to monitor, but I prefer to wait for a clearer inflection or a more attractive re-entry level.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the newest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street on Friday posted its best week since early August, driven by a combination of strong earnings, optimism over trade relations between the U.S. and China, and a reprieve for market participants starved of economic data during the ongoing government shutdown.

Arguably, the main event of the week was Friday's long-awaited consumer price index (CPI) report for September. Both the headline and core CPI were softer than anticipated on a M/M basis, with the latter rising at its slowest pace since June.

The CPI print reinforced expectations that the Federal Reserve would deliver another 25 basis point interest rate cut at its monetary policy committee meeting next week. Meanwhile, the data could be the last inflation indicator for a while, as the White House said the October CPI report likely won't be released.

Meanwhile, on Thursday, a meeting between President Donald Trump and China's Xi Jinping was confirmed. The two leaders will meet during Trump's upcoming visit to Asia, amid simmering trade tensions between the world's two largest economies.

Turning to the earnings season, tech heavyweights Netflix (NFLX) and Tesla (TSLA) were the highlights. The streaming giant's stock slumped after its quarterly profit took a hit from an expense stemming from a dispute with Brazilian tax authorities. On the other hand, shares of Tesla (TSLA) rose after the electric vehicle maker delivered record quarterly revenue.

Notably, shares of General Motors (GM) and Ford (F) soared after the legacy automakers impressed investors with their results.

For the week, the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) gained +1.9%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) added +2.2%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced +2.3%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: The Coiled Spring I'm Betting On - My Top Picks For America's Next Housing Boom
data.description:
Introduction

On October 12, I wrote an article titled "Why I Will Be Betting Big On America’s Most Critical Market." That market is so critical because it affects every single American, as it’s about housing. While food may be a bit more important, I think it’s fair to say that housing is the most critical market when we combine necessity and the total dollar value, which is likely why the president is so focused on it.

Earlier this week, CNN Business covered the president’s comments in an article with the title "Trump is blaming homebuilders for the housing shortage. But the problem goes much deeper."

Obviously, I know what some of you may be thinking. It’s a bad source, as CNN and Trump are known to have a somewhat complicated relationship, to put it mildly. There's even a lawsuit named "CNN v. Trump."

The only reason I’m mentioning this is to tell you there’s not going to be any bias in this article, as you may be used to in my articles. See, one thing the article brought up was the fact that President Trump is blaming homebuilders for affordability issues, as they are sitting on millions of empty lots. As there’s a shortage of homes, one can make the case that something weird is going on, for lack of a better description. In fact, new construction never recovered from its Great Financial Crisis peak levels, when the market was red-hot.

While it would obviously help prices a lot if every builder were to fill in empty lots with new houses, it’s not that easy, as regulations, labor shortages, and financing costs are issues.

As long as mortgage rates are north of 6%, it prices many out of the market when adding elevated home prices in most regions of the United States:

In general, builders need to be careful, as overbuilding hurts margins. Poor margins result in lower earnings, which keep investors at bay, scare rating agencies, and make running the business even harder. You know, capitalism stuff.

Especially in a market where financing is becoming an issue, randomly building more homes is not the easy solution that we may expect it to be. Here’s some proof from the same article:

A study from the National Association of Homebuilders (NAHB) found that nearly 25% of the price of a typical newly built single-family home is due to regulations imposed by state, local and federal governments. The NAHB has lobbied against what it calls “regulatory burdens” around building homes.

If land-use regulations were relaxed, an extra 2.5 million more housing units would likely be added over the next decade - eliminating about two-thirds of the estimated housing shortage, according to an analysis by Goldman Sachs. - CNN Business

Now, regardless of one’s politics, which truly don’t matter in this context, this market has become a massive coiled spring opportunity, where the only thing keeping a huge wave of buyers at bay is the mix of unfavorable regulations and elevated rates.

That’s why I wrote about housing earlier this month, as I believe being prepared for this opportunity is absolutely crucial. If I’m even remotely right and the current administration manages to use deregulations and lower rates to unlock demand ("running it hot"), we could encounter some of the biggest opportunities in more than a decade in housing.

Hence, in the second part, I’ll update my thesis using important new developments and shed more light on actionable ideas. After all, a good thesis is worthless if we cannot turn it into a profitable investment.

So, let’s dive in!

A Coiled Spring Waiting To Pop

To summarize my thesis, I believe it takes just a bit of a tailwind to get homebuyers to rush in after the market has been stuck for more than three years.

Now, I found more evidence of my thesis, as The Wall Street Journal published a piece earlier this month with the near-perfect title "Homeowners Are Pouncing on the Tiniest Drop in Mortgage Rates."

If that doesn’t perfectly embody my coiled spring thesis, I don’t know what does.

One of the most important facts from the article is that a 0.3-point decline in the 30-year mortgage rate to 6.26%, which is the lowest number in 11 months, triggered an 80% boost in refinancing demand. It perfectly shows just how much demand is on the sidelines.

These developments aren’t just key for the housing market, but also for consumer sentiment, which has been a mess for a very long time, as we can see below. Needless to say, as all consumers need a roof above their head, housing is very much tied to consumer confidence and general spending.

The interesting thing is that 90% of all mortgage refinancings were from loans originated in 2023 and 2024. These are homeowners who have little to no equity, high rates on their loans, and a very high desire to lower their costs as much as possible.

To give you an idea of how big the impact could be, the same article (using Intercontinental Exchange numbers) noted that 3.1 million homeowners could reduce their rates by more than 75 basis points and save roughly $400 per month on mortgage payments.

If the 30-year rate were to drop to 6.0%, that number would rise to nearly 6.0 million mortgages. I’m not guaranteeing anyone that the Fed will be able to achieve a substantial decline in mortgage rates, but as I expect interference to improve liquidity and lower long-term bond rates, I think we could see a move toward 5.0%, which would provide substantial benefits to more than 12.5 million mortgages and unleash thousands in potential savings on larger homes. The impact on that could be massive.

In other words, new data confirms my thesis, as there are at least three key takeaways here:

  • Borrowers are extremely eager to refinance. This is obvious, given what most have gone through in recent years.
  • Borrowers are extremely sensitive to changes in long-term interest rates, which is a good thing, as a larger decline in long-term rates could have a huge impact on spending in the housing sector.
  • Even a small decline in rates is already having a major impact on housing demand.

So, what does this mean for investors?

Some Of My Favorite Housing-Related Stocks

I’m a "picks and shovels" guy.

While most of you may be familiar with that term, it’s basically a way to invest in the underlying technology/supplies without having to pick winners when it comes to the final product. For example, it’s why I own Comfort Systems USA (FIX), which produces HVAC and related products for data centers. To me, it doesn’t matter what kind of chips are used for the data centers or which hyperscaler uses them. All that matters is that I’m right about the data center buildout.

In my prior housing article, I brought up a number of companies I really like. These include Carlisle Companies (CSL), which is a supplier of construction materials with a focus on replacement and remodeling operations. While it mainly serves commercial operations (>80% of total revenue in 2024), it benefits from elevated pent-up demand in commercial renovation with an increasing focus on advanced materials that support weatherproofing and insulation.

Especially at its current valuation, CSL is one of my favorite investments, which I expect to make its way to $800 per share based on its Vision 2030 plans of at least $40 in EPS and a 20x multiple.

The company also stands out with a return on invested capital of roughly 29.0%, one of the best numbers among all major industrials, and proof of very effective management. Generally speaking, I like this space, which is why I also brought up QXO (QXO), which is a company I will focus on a lot more in the future, as it’s the latest project of Brad Jacobs, the billionaire founder of businesses like XPO (XPO) and United Rentals (URI), among others.

QXO is a building material distributor that aims to grow through organic and acquired growth and boost efficiencies through its operating model and integration of AI. I do not own it, but I have to say that it’s very high on my buy list right now.

A similar yet different peer is Boise Cascade (BCC), which owns sawmills, making it a major supplier of homebuilders and a distribution business in most major housing hotspots that distributes other critical supplies for new construction and remodeling operations.

I’m adding a new pick to this list, which is TopBuild (BLD), a company that has become a leader in residential, commercial, and industrial insulation and building materials. It just announced the takeover of Specialty Products and Insulation in an all-cash $1 billion deal that will further expand its footprint in specialty and maintenance services and goods, allowing the company to benefit from both new construction demand and the increasing installed base of aging homes that will see an acceleration in maintenance demand.

Currently trading at roughly 22x earnings, analysts (FactSet) see a path to accelerating EPS growth of 15% in 2027. I expect this to happen sooner and believe the BLD stock price will see a major breakout over the next 12 months.

Now, if we add an "R" to the ticker above, we get Builders FirstSource (BLDR), which is another supplier I really like. The company, which is headquartered in Texas, is the biggest supplier of building products and prefabricated components that serves 48 of the biggest 50 housing markets across 43 states.

The company has an on-the-road sales force of roughly 2,600 people, with an average employee tenure of more than 13 years. In general, it has great customer service, as it enjoys a 95% retention rate, while 85% of its buyers purchase in at least two categories.

Additionally, it has a healthy leverage ratio of 1.5x, no maturing debt until 2030, and a focus on accretive M&A, which I expect to heat up as well, as all major players have understood how big an opportunity inorganic growth in a highly fragmented market can be, especially when new tools like AI can help them to better integrate new businesses.

Just like the others I mentioned so far, these companies are in a prime spot to benefit from higher building demand, pricing, and what I expect to become red-hot remodeling demand due to more than a decade of lacking supply.

Other ways to play this are DIY and PRO-focused retailers like Home Depot (HD) and Lowe’s (LOW), homebuilders with a focus on affordable options and high-demand markets in the South like D.R. Horton, or general transportation companies that will support these supply chains, including Union Pacific (UNP), although UNP is highly diversified, which means general manufacturing indicators are more important than housing indicators.

Other companies I have to mention are Trex Company (TREX), which produces composite decking. Trading at 23.4x earnings with double-digit expected EPS growth even in an environment of elevated rates, I think this one can take off meaningfully.

I also like Pool Corporation (POOL), which is a distributor (did I mention I like distributors?) of pool-related products. After a downtrend that started as soon as housing demand peaked during the pandemic, I think this one is due for a strong rebound in the years ahead.

Needless to say, as I could spend just a few lines on all companies, investors have to do their own due diligence before deploying a single penny in this space.

My main point is that we’re dealing with one of the biggest coiled spring opportunities in housing in more than a decade. If the government and the Fed are even remotely able to get rates down and "run it hot," I think distributors, suppliers, and select homebuilders are the place to be.

I would be a little careful with multi-family REITs like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) and Camden Property Trust (CPT). Although I consider them some of the best REITs for housing income, I think a surge in new supply could be hurtful to their pricing power (rent).

To me, the biggest opportunities are further upstream.

Takeaway

The housing market has turned into a massive coiled spring opportunity and one of my most important theses. Not only is it the biggest and most critical market for most Americans, but it is also the epicenter of measures from both the Fed and the government.

As I expect we’re in a scenario where the two aforementioned players are "running it hot," pressuring rates and loosening regulations, I believe we could be looking at a massive opportunity in housing-related stocks, including suppliers, distributors, homebuilders, retailers, and others.

I own Carlisle, Home Depot, and transportation stocks, yet I’m not nearly done buying.

So, stay tuned for that!

Risks To My Thesis

On top of general recession risks, any headwind causing rates to remain elevated could keep this market stuck for much longer. This could be runaway inflation forcing the Fed to hike rates again, or any political headwinds that shift the focus away from housing. I don’t expect it to happen, yet we should always be on the lookout for these risks, as they could ruin the opportunity to generate alpha in this space.

data.title: Consistent Income Generators On Sale: 2 Cheap Dividend Stocks That Pay Big
data.description:
Introduction

As someone who plans to live off dividends in the not too distant future, my goal is to buy stocks that not only reward me with dividends, but will likely do so for the foreseeable future. Ten or twenty years from now, I expect my portfolio to be comprised of the same companies.

This is unless those companies' fundamentals change. As someone who takes a quality-over-quantity approach, I think my current holdings can continue providing income. This is why it's prudent for income-focused investors to do their research and be careful not to chase stocks with shiny yields.

While investing in itself is risky, having a strategy is key for success long-term. If you're someone who also is looking for reliable income generators, these two cheap dividend stocks may be what you need for your portfolio.

AI Gets All The Hype

In late 2025, all the headlines point to the future of artificial intelligence. As a result, this has led to frothy valuations in the market. Particularly, from those that benefit greatly from the adoption of AI.

On the flip side, this has led to some fundamentally-sound stocks being left behind. There's always opportunity; the key is one has to look for it. Below you can see the Technology sector (XLK) has significantly outperformed the Financial (XLF) and Industrial (XLI) sectors, up nearly 24% in comparison to single-digits for the other two.

Macro uncertainty has also led to underperformance in certain sectors, particularly those impacted by consumer spending. But as previously mentioned, this should be viewed as a potential opportunity. Especially, if you're a long-term investor.

So, without further ado, here's two fundamentally-sound stocks long-term investors may find attractive.

#1 U.S. Bancorp (USB) - Solid 4.36% Yield

First on the list is regional bank, USB. I can understand why investors are skeptical about investing in financial stocks. Especially, considering the current level of economic uncertainty. But U.S. Bancorp is one of the top 10 largest banks in the U.S. with over $500 billion in assets.

Understandably, the real estate market also has investors worried. So, banks may not be on everyone's buy list currently as they are impacted due to their residential & mortgage lending practices.

Although not large enough to be considered a Globally Systemically Important Bank, USB is large enough that it's highly unlikely they will fail. Or that they will see a bank run like Silicon Valley Bank did back in early 2023.

In their latest quarter, the regional bank giant saw solid growth across the board. Their bottom line managed to beat by a solid $0.09, amounting to $1.22 for Q3. Revenue also beat by $150 million, and both saw growth from the prior quarter and year.

EPS climbed from $1.11 in Q2 and $1.03 in the prior year's quarter, while their top line of $7.3 billion grew 9.5% year-over-year. Both net interest and non-interest income grew to $4.25 billion and $3.08 billion, up from $4.17 billion and $2.7 billion, respectively.

Their CET1 ratio was up 40 basis points year-over-year to 10.9%. This was in comparison to their largest peer and the gold standard of U.S. banks, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) whose CET1 ratio stood at 14.8%. Both were well-above the Federal Reserve's minimum of 4.5%, a testament to both's fundamental strength.

Some investors may attribute USB's performance to fresh blood with a new CEO back in April. But with only one earnings report under her belt, it's too early to attribute the bank's success to new leadership. But her 30 years of experience in financial services should serve the bank well going forward.

U.S. Bancorp's balance sheet was also solid, with a 1.8% increase in average deposits to $512 billion. Loan growth was 1.4%, and loan & investment portfolio yields saw an increase from 3.20% to 3.26%. This was a result of an improved portfolio mix.

But more attractive are U.S. Bancorp's dividend yield of 4.36% and valuation. Currently, the yield is a well-covered payout ratio below 50%. The company increased the dividend by 4% in July to $0.52 a share.

At the time of writing, USB is down close to 9% in the past year, impacted by macro uncertainty from higher for longer interest rates. But as rates come down gradually, I expect they could see solid upside over the next year or two.

Their forward P/E sits slightly below the sector median's 10.56x. Wall Street currently rates them a buy with a price target of $55.67, roughly 17% upside. While I expect them to trade in the $46 - $48 range for the near-term, in the next 12 months USB should see tailwinds from lower base rates and trade above $50.

#2 Verizon (VZ) - Near 7% Yield

Second on the list is a much more popular stock that also had a recent leadership change. Earlier this month, Verizon announced their new CEO, PayPal's (PYPL) former Dan Schulman.

As an off-and-on Verizon shareholder, I think the leadership change was needed. Verizon's acquisitions have seemingly been good, but haven't paid off or given investor's enough confidence to move the needle.

Throw in the high-interest rate environment & telecom companies' high debt loads, and Verizon has been a bit of a disappointment. The stock is down over 7% in the past year. But with interest rates expected to come down gradually over the next year or two, this should provide tailwinds.

Their acquisitions seem strategic and could set the telecom giant up for long-term success to compete with peer T-Mobile (TMUS). But this will all depend on the execution of the new CEO.

Until then, I expect them to remain range-bound in the $38 - $42 range. Earlier this month, VZ announced the acquisition of Starry to expand their broadband footprint in major areas like Los Angeles, Boston, and New York. They also signed a deal with AST SpaceMobile (ASTS).

This was after peer T-Mobile announced a deal with Starlink. Both the acquisition and partnership for Verizon are expected to take place sometime in 2026. But the question is: How will these acquisitions make an impact? I think Verizon is doing the right things, especially considering the tough economic backdrop.

Verizon has to execute on gaining subscriber growth, something that TMUS has been executing well on. To combat this, Verizon at the beginning of the month announced a new plan to gain subscribers from peers T-Mobile and AT&T (T).

Verizon expects to give customers a better deal than they're currently getting with TMUS & T by offering cheaper plans. I think this is a strategic move. Especially, considering the financial challenges consumers have faced with higher-for-longer interest rates. Consumers want value, and Verizon is looking to capitalize.

Who wouldn't want a cheaper bill? Cellphones are our lifelines. And even if one can get $5 or $10 off a month, this adds up over time. Especially, considering how inflation has resulted in increased costs in recent years.

Verizon is slated to announce earnings next week. Analysts' expect their bottom line to retract $0.03 from Q2 and revenue to decline slightly from $34.5 billion. Unless VZ experiences a steep decline in subscribers, I expect EPS in a range of $1.20 - $1.23. And revenue to climb slightly from Q2.

If the stock does miss or earnings decline, I expect volatility and the share price to fall below $38, close to $36 - $37 a share. But Verizon has been making progress, albeit slow. In Q2, net debt declined year-over-year from $122.8 billion to $116 billion, bringing leverage to 2.3x.

Free cash flow also grew, enhancing the company's dividend safety. Although, this was never in danger as VZ has maintained a conservative payout ratio. Currently, this is below 60%.

This gives them ample retained cash flow for acquisitions and a potential buyback program. This is something I've discussed previously that could help give shareholders more confidence. And while this could happen in the not too distant future, I don't expect one soon due to their string of acquisitions and debt load.

But while you wait for management to execute and acquisitions to become accretive, investors get paid a 6.84% yield that's well-covered by cash flow while they wait.

A stock like Verizon has its place in an income-focused portfolio. And at a price close to $38 a share, I think the stock is attractive with upside potential in the next year or two.

Currently, their forward P/E is closer to 8x and well below the sector median. Offering double-digit upside to their price target, I think they can break the $50 barrier once rates come down and if they can see subscriber growth.

Risks & Takeaway

Due to macro uncertainty and their respective sectors, U.S. Bancorp and Verizon could see increased volatility in the near-term. USB recently saw a spike in volatility as a result of loan defaults by regional bank peers. And this can carry over and will likely keep investors cautious.

If Verizon misses on earnings or sees a decline in subscribers in Q3, I expect more volatility and the stock to fall closer to the $37 level. However, this would be a great buying opportunity, as I think VZ's long-term fundamentals remain attractive.

If you're an income-oriented investor looking for fundamentally sound income generators with attractive valuations, consider these two for your portfolio.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Foreshocks
data.description:
Future readers will struggle to comprehend how crazily unsettled things became – the markets, finance, economic function, societies, politics, and geopolitics. Stocks at new records this week, though not without more tremors forewarning of a major quake. Most have grown comfortable with, and dismissive of, the stirring drumbeat of Foreshocks. But some of us are darned worried about increasingly fragile fault lines and the eventuality of “the big one.”

The dichotomy between late-cycle speculative and liquidity excess versus a deteriorating Credit environment was front and center this week. From central bankers to bankers to Credit investors and rating agencies - perceptions are shifting. Discussions of weak underwriting and problematic market structure are no more confined to private conversations. The word “Bubble” is no longer lunatic fringe. Newfound scrutiny and resulting Credit tightening will prove problematic for scores of vulnerable borrowers and economic prospects more generally. But major impacts materialize over time.

Meanwhile, liquidity overabundance persists in key markets. In the near term, incipient credit problems have triggered a bond rally and declining market yields. Global markets remain highly over-liquefied. It’s as if festering U.S. Credit issues and rallying Treasuries have provided a meaningful jolt to global bond leveraging and liquidity. Notable liquidity-generating short squeezes have unfolded in U.S., French and UK bonds. Additionally, a more general unwind of hedges (against rising global market yields) provides a further liquidity boost.

The Japanese yen dropped another 1.5% this week, boosting October losses versus the dollar to 3.2%. The weaker yen and the prospect of a more dovish BOJ could promote aggressive yen “carry trade” leveraging and resulting liquidity creation.

October 23 – Bloomberg (Liza Tetley and Nishant Kumar): “Assets in the global hedge fund industry have surged to a record $5 trillion as investors poured money into alternatives and funds posted solid gains. Hedge funds saw net inflows of nearly $34 billion in the three months through September, according to… Hedge Fund Research… That’s the most in any quarter since 2007, before the global financial crisis. The surge in assets also came from investment performance, with total returns across all strategies averaging 5.4% over the quarter.”

It's worth noting that Money Market Fund Assets (MMFA) inflated another $30 billion this past week to a record $7.398 TN (1-yr growth $900bn, or 13.7%). MMFA surged $191 billion over the past eight weeks, further extending historic monetary inflation that I associate with the expansion of leveraged speculation and attendant “repo” borrowings. This dynamic has expanded into a global phenomenon over recent years.

Indicative of liquidity excess, major equities index gains this week included South Korea 5.1% (up 64.3% y-t-d), Japan 3.6% (23.6%), Hong Kong 3.6% (30.4%), and China 2.9% (17.9%). Europe’s gains included the UK 3.1% (18.0%), Germany 1.7% (21.8%), Spain 1.7% (36.8%), Italy 1.7% (24.3%), and Sweden 3.2% (12.2%). Notable outperforming, the (Hong Kong) Hang Seng China Financial Index’s 4.2% surge increased y-t-d gains to 31.9%. This week’s advances pushed Europe’s STOXX 600 Bank Index to a 43.4% y-t-d gain, and Italian bank stock gains to 45.9%.

Lately, not much seems to irk Treasuries, including the Atlanta Fed GDPNow forecast, which remains elevated at 3.89%. The preliminary October Manufacturing PMI was reported Friday at a slightly ahead-of-expectations 52.2, as the Services PMI added a point to a robust 55.2 (3-month high and 2nd highest reading over 10 months). At 54.2, the composite PMI New Orders subcomponent was the strongest back to December 2024.

Friday’s slightly better-than-forecast CPI report (up 3.0% y-o-y) notwithstanding, elevated inflation risks live on. Many Americans are now learning of significant increases to 2026 health insurance premiums, which go along with rising home and auto insurance costs. Rising prices for so many things (i.e., imported goods, electricity, beef, coffee, food generally…) explain the (October final) University of Michigan Consumer Survey’s highly elevated 4.6% expected one-year inflation rate – along with 3.9% five-to-10-year expected inflation (rising from preliminary 3.7%).

Friday afternoon Drudge headlines: “Govt Claims Inflation ‘3%’”; “Real or Fake?”; “Companies Shielded Buyers from Tariffs. Not for long…” “Poll: Cost-of-Living Worries Haunt Americans…” and “Beef Prices Soar…” From Bloomberg: “US Inflation Comes in Soft, Building Case for More Fed Cuts.”

Treasury yields have declined 16 bps this month – and are now down 44 bps from July highs – to trade at one-year lows. Market inflation expectations (5-yr “breakeven” rate) closed Monday at 2.32% - down 23 bps from the August high to near the low since April’s downdraft. Interestingly, the five-year breakeven rate was back up to 2.40% by week’s end.

Replaying previous Bubble cycle dynamics, lower market yields have helped ameliorate incipient Credit concerns. Expectations for a more assertive Fed rate-cutting cycle support the bullish narrative. From my perspective, fledgling issues at the “periphery,” ensuring looser policies, are underpinning the booming “core.” Investment-grade spreads to Treasuries at 75 bps, up only three bps from the multi-year lows of three weeks ago. These are below where they began 2025 (80bps) and significantly down from April highs (119bps).

Meanwhile, “periphery” leveraged loan prices have barely recovered from First Brands-related selling. Beginning the month at 97.06 (down from July’s 97.58 peak) and trading to 96.35 on October 14th, leveraged loan prices ended the week at 96.55.

October 24 – Bloomberg (Jeannine Amodeo): “US leveraged-loan activity rebounded some from one of the slowest weeks of 2025, but this week is still poised to fall short of $10 billion in volume as leveraged - finance activity remains muted following a sizzling summer… Amid the market’s broader sluggishness, loan funds had their first back-to-back weekly outflows since April, LSEG Lipper data show.”

October 24 – Bloomberg (Kyle Ashworth and Dorothea Quallis): “The bankruptcy of First Brands and Tricolor Holdings has forced leveraged loan investors to pare risk. The Bloomberg US Leveraged Loan Index (Loan Index) is heading for its first loss since April, as companies and consumers buckle under the impact of tariffs and rising costs. The selective default rate for the benchmark has quadrupled in the past two years. Managers of collateralized loan obligations are responding to accelerating credit rating downgrades by selling distressed issuers.”

October 24 – Bloomberg (Gowri Gurumurthy): “US junk bonds are headed for modest weekly gains after yields fell by a mere two bps in the last four sessions and risk premium by just five basis points… The US junk bond rally also lost some momentum as jittery investors pulled $970m from high-yield mutual funds, excluding exchange-traded funds, for the week ended Wednesday… the second straight week of cash outflows from US junk bond mutual funds... The two weeks combined resulted in an outflow of $1.73b… After a sudden burst of issuance this summer and a supply boom that made it the busiest September on record, the primary market has slowed, with issuance almost grinding to a halt.”

October 20 – Bloomberg (Rachel Graf and Olivia Fishlow): “Exchange-traded funds that hold bundles of corporate loans last week notched their first outflows since April, in the latest sign that investor concerns over credit quality are mounting. ETFs of collateralized loan obligations experienced an outflow of about $516 million last week, marking the first investor exodus in about six months, analysts at JPMorgan… wrote… That compares to a weekly average of about $421 million of inflows over the past year…”

While mitigated in the markets by liquidity abundance and exuberance, there are important indications of heightened risk aversion and tighter underwriting for high-risk lending. It is too early to assert a decisive reversal of speculative finance away from the “periphery,” though it’s moving in that direction.

October 20 – Financial Times (Ortenca Aliaj and Robert Smith): “JPMorgan… has said that credit worries from the First Brands and Tricolor bankruptcies have driven up banks’ funding costs, with investors concerned about lenders’ hidden exposure to private capital firms and hedge funds. The JPMorgan analysts made Monday’s comments about the implied cost of equity after last week’s sell-off of bank stocks, which was sparked by disclosures by two US regional lenders that they were exposed to alleged fraud by borrowers. The high-profile collapses of car parts maker First Brands Group and subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings within weeks of each other have highlighted the complex and often opaque financial arrangements between banks and ‘non-depository financial institutions’. JPMorgan said banks’ lack of transparency about lending to such so-called NDFIs — a broad category that includes private equity groups, private credit firms and hedge funds — was pushing investors to demand higher compensation for owning their shares. ‘The recent global banks sell-off was triggered by poor risk management, in our view, as shown by First Brands supply-chain exposures but more importantly, very poor disclosure in relation to [NDFIs] globally across the banking system,’ JPMorgan analysts said…”

“High-profile collapses… have highlighted the complex and often opaque financial arrangements between banks and ‘non-depository financial institutions’.” “Lack of transparency,” “poor risk management” and “very poor disclosure.” A key passage from the above Bloomberg article:

“Regulators have become increasingly concerned about the interconnectedness of banks and NDFIs. The IMF earlier this month warned of the need for greater oversight of the sector. US and European banks are estimated to have $4.5tn of exposure to the wider category of non-bank financial institutions, equal on average to approximately 9% of total loan books. The collapse of First Brands and Tricolor has shown how banks remain exposed to corporate crashes even when a company’s borrowing is largely outside the domain of traditional bank lending.”

Breakneck growth in leveraged lending, “private Credit,” and “junk” issuance constitutes a historic boom in high-risk lending. It has been conspicuous – a Bubble that those responsible for safeguarding financial and system stability have disregarded. As is too often the case, such excess is initially dismissed as fleeting financial folly without systemic ramifications – only for Bubbles to then inflate to the point where no one dares risk triggering a destabilizing bust. Behind the scenes, there must have been heightened concerns, discussions, and perhaps even consternation – which finally enter public discourse after widening cracks reveal roach colonies.

October 21 – Financial Times (Martin Arnold and Ortenca Aliaj): “Bank of England governor Andrew Bailey has said ‘alarm bells’ are ringing over risky lending in the private credit markets following the collapse of First Brands and Tricolor, as he drew a parallel with practices before the 2008 financial crisis. The comments from Bailey underline the concern among regulators that the rapid demise of US car parts supplier First Brands and subprime auto lender Tricolor in recent weeks are a sign of financial strains in the complex private credit markets. Referring to how repackaged financial products have in the past obscured the risk of the underlying assets, Bailey said: ‘We certainly are beginning to see, for instance, what used to be called slicing and dicing and tranching of loan structures going on, and if you were involved before the financial crisis then alarm bells start going off at that point.’”

I am compelled to further underscore Martin Arnold and Ortenca Aliaj’s important article:

“Private credit markets have become a critical source of funding for consumers and businesses as traditional banks have retreated since the financial crisis.”

“Wall Street’s practice of packaging subprime mortgages into asset-backed bonds fuelled the 2008 financial crisis, with years of loose lending standards leading to a crash in the value of these assets when US house prices fell. In the run-up to the crisis, bankers and investors had regarded many such complicated financial products as virtually riskless. The perception encouraged large institutions to borrow heavily against their holdings…”

Andrew Bailey: “If you go back to before the financial crisis when we were having a debate about subprime mortgages in the US, people were telling us it was too small to be systemic. That was the wrong call. I’m not saying therefore the call should be the same this time but it underlines why the question is apposite.”

“Bailey’s comments follow a warning last week from the IMF that US and European banks’ $4.5tn exposure to hedge funds, private credit groups and other non-bank financial institutions could amplify any downturn and transmit stress to the wider financial system.”

“Sarah Breeden, deputy governor for financial stability… ‘We can see the vulnerabilities here, the opacity, the leverage, the weak underwriting standards, the interconnections. We can see parallels with the global financial crisis. What we don’t know is how macro-significant those issues are.’”

Undoubtedly, “those issues” are of utmost macro-significance. High-risk “private Credit” is said to have ballooned to $1.7 TN, though it’s surely much larger. Besides, “private Credit” is only one component of this cycle’s high-risk lending boom.

Most importantly, this most protracted Credit Bubble has altered market, financial and economic structures. In short, it has played an instrumental role in fostering today’s unprecedented wealth disparities. This structure creates unappreciated systemic fragilities, including that the more unfortunate that have taken on so much debt during this cycle. Then there are the financially fortunate that have benefited tremendously from asset inflation, whose borrowing and spending is now integral to sustaining asset Bubbles and the U.S. Bubble Economy more generally.

October 22 – Reuters (Arasu Kannagi Basil): “U.S. banks’ loans to private credit providers have surged to nearly $300 billion, Moody’s said…, and the ratings agency warned that smaller lenders could face heightened risks if underwriting standards weaken. Loans to non-depository financial institutions (NDFIs) are now 10.4% of total bank loans, nearly three times the 3.6% exposure a decade ago, the report said. The aggressive growth outpaced all other lending activities since 2016, it added… Besides exposure to private credit providers, there was a further $285 billion in loans to private equity funds as of June, and $340 billion in unutilized commitments available to these borrowers…”

October 23 – Bloomberg (Jeannine Amodeo): “Banks are preparing to launch a $38 billion debt offering as soon as Monday that will help fund data centers tied to Oracle Corp. in what would be the largest such deal for artificial intelligence infrastructure to come to market, according to people with knowledge... JPMorgan... and Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group are among banks leading the deal…”

A Bloomberg Intelligence headline earlier in the week: “AI Needs Push 2025 Tech Bond Issuance Toward Record $200 billion.”

The ongoing booming “core” has so far lavishly accommodated AI arms race financing demands. Importantly, the all-powerful Bubble at the “core” of AI finance continues to underpin riskier AI borrowings, albeit through leveraged loans, “private Credit,” bank lending and such. Moreover, AI manias and Bubbles have become integral to the markets (equities and debt) and U.S. and global economies more generally. These dynamics create extraordinary vulnerability to any surprise market de-risking/deleveraging and associated liquidity issues. There is no escaping today’s reality of unprecedented speculative leverage – especially prominent throughout equites (tech in particular), crypto, corporate Credit, derivatives and, of course, the Trillions of egregiously levered “basis trades” and “carry trades.”

Under the headline, “Fed’s Portfolio Unwind Gains Urgency as Markets Flash Warnings:”

October 24 – Bloomberg (Alexandra Harris): “When Federal Reserve officials meet next week to decide whether to cut interest rates again, they’ll face another question that’s becoming increasingly urgent — how soon they should stop shrinking the bank’s $6.6 trillion portfolio of securities. Money markets have been flashing warnings for several weeks that the process, known as quantitative tightening, may have run its course. Now, Wall Street strategists say, stress signals have gathered such momentum that the Fed may be forced to end QT as soon as this month. Since the central bank started reducing its portfolio in June 2022, more than $2 trillion in funds have left the financial system. This has nearly emptied its main liquidity barometer — the reverse repurchase facility — just as a deluge of short-term debt issuance is luring more cash away. In turn, a variety of interest rates used among banks to borrow and lend to each other have risen, while a tool introduced to dampen market pressures has seen regular use over the past week.”

More from Alexandra Harris’ insightful article: Quoting Mark Cabana, head of US interest rate strategy at Bank of America: “You can make the case that there’s not just a risk that the Fed is at the in-between stage, but they’re there. There’s a very small echo of 2019 and that the Fed over-drained cash and likely knows it.”

From Cabana and colleague Katie Craig: “Money markets at current or higher levels should signal to the Fed that reserves are no longer ‘abundant. By some metrics the Fed may also judge that reserves are no longer ‘ample’.”

And from JPMorgan strategists, led by Teresa Ho: “The September 2019 turmoil revealed that the Fed has less tolerance for volatility in the fed funds rate and the ‘constellation of money market funds,’ committing the same mistake twice could have significant ramifications.”

Recent unusual upward pressure on overnight “repo” rates has Wall Street clamoring for the end of “QT” – the winding down of Fed balance sheet contraction in preparation for QE. Repo market instability in the summer of 2019 saw the Fed aggressively restart QE months ahead of the pandemic. It’s worth noting that the Fed’s balance sheet bottomed at $3.76 TN in late-August 2019 - ending the year at $4.166 TN and on its way to $7.165 TN by early June and an April 2022 peak of $8.965 TN. Fed Assets are today $6.59 TN, which is apparently, in the eyes of Wall Street, increasingly insufficient.

Recent “repo” market tightness is a critical piece of today’s puzzle. Is it, as it was in the summer of 2019, an initial sign of vulnerability and risk aversion within a leveraged speculating community way too far out on its skis?

October 24 – Bloomberg (Justina Lee and Denitsa Tsekova): “Behind the scenes of placid Wall Street markets, drama is unfolding for some heavy-hitting professional investors. Quantitative funds are suffering this month amid reversals in crowded and previously money-minting positions. The risks in stretched momentum trades were laid bare this week in sessions like Wednesday, when high-flying gold, tech shares and crypto were torpedoed all at once. Quant long-short funds are down 1.7% in October, posting their first losses since another big bout of volatility in July… The $20 billion Renaissance Institutional Equities Fund lost about 15% through Oct. 10… In one brutal example, a long-short Morgan Stanley basket tracking momentum stocks slid 11.3% over the five days ending Wednesday, the sharpest decline since March… A Goldman basket of the market’s most-shorted stocks saw its October month-to-date gains reach 21% at one point — likely hurting hedge funds…”

From the above article, a quote from Richard Craib, founder of hedge fund Numerai: “‘Junk rallies’ hurt quants because they are often short low-quality stocks and long high-quality stocks. If the damage gets big enough, quant funds start to delever to cover their shorts, making matters worse and leading to a deleveraging cascade.”

The Goldman Sachs Most Short Index jumped 2.5% Friday, extending an 11-session stretch of remarkable volatility. Over the previous 10 sessions, the index rose 2.0%, dropped 4.5%, fell 2.7%, surged 4.1%, dropped 2.8%, sank 4.7%, jumped 4.3%, rose 2.4%, surged 5.8%, and sank 4.1%. Not healthy.

Market action suggests quant fund deleveraging. There is potential for “a deleveraging cascade.” But in speculative marketplaces – the current backdrop in particular – signs of a short squeeze provide a rallying cry to aggressively buy stocks, ETFs, and, for the best bang for the buck, call options. Free money. At October 16th highs, the GS Short Index had surged 37% in six weeks - and was 122% above April lows. If one was envisaging a scenario for a major top, we’re seeing it.

Between extraordinary happenings in Credit, “repo,” “quants,” short stocks, the metals markets, and global equities, there are clearly acute instabilities festering below the markets’ “resilient” veneer. Pressures are building. I’m on liquidity and “earthquake” watch. It has that feel of de-risking and deleveraging Foreshocks.

Between the Fed and the Trump/Xi meeting, next week will be another interesting one. I find myself this evening pondering whether the President’s deployment of our largest aircraft carrier and strike group as a show of force in Latin America, new Russian sanctions, and his Canada Reagan “fraud” tantrum have Xi Jinping either more or less inclined to give Trump a trade negotiation win next week in South Korea. Things get more alarming and destructive by the week.
