Many pay higher deductibles that can result in thousands of dollars in out-of-pocket costs if they become seriously ill or have an accident.”

October 24 – New York Times (Lydia DePillis and Kailyn Rhone): “Since the Trump administration started imposing steep tariffs on goods from the rest of the world, the Treasury Department has been taking in about $30 billion a month in customs duties. On paper, American companies pay those bills... But figuring who ultimately absorbs the costs is more complicated… Companies had passed along about 37% of new tariffs to consumers, forced 9% onto their suppliers and absorbed 51% through August, according to Goldman Sachs. That is a big hit to shoppers’ wallets… But it is milder than it would have been if companies were charging consumers as much as they had at the same point in the last burst of tariffs during President Trump’s first term. Mostly that is because the tariffs are much higher and more widespread than in 2018, making them too difficult for consumers to digest all at once.”

October 23 – Axios (Jim VandeHei and Mike Allen): “America faces an economy-shaping energy jam: We’ve never needed more of it more urgently to meet rising consumer and business demand. We produce and export more energy than at any point in history. But the data centers powering the AI boom suck up so much energy, so fast, it’s increasingly jacking up prices for nearby ordinary residents. This dynamic — surging demand and finite supply — is playing out in community after community. It creates a difficult tension for President Trump, who wants both to accelerate AI and slow energy spikes for consumers. Campaigning in a barn in Pennsylvania last year, Trump said: ‘Your energy bill, within 12 months, will be cut in half. ...We have more energy under our feet than any other country. I call it liquid gold.’ Yet the AI frenzy is juicing demand for energy-hungry data centers, which have raised wholesale electricity prices for tens of thousands of cities, towns and suburbs around the U.S."

October 24 – Associated Press (Fatima Hussein): “The Social Security Administration’s annual cost-of-living adjustment will go up by 2.8% in 2026, translating to an average increase of more than $56 for retirees every month… The benefits increase for nearly 71 million Social Security recipients will go into effect beginning in January. And increased payments to nearly 7.5 million people receiving Supplemental Security Income will begin on Dec. 31.”

October 23 – Bloomberg (Ilena Peng): “Coffee futures rose to a fresh record… as US tariffs and crop concerns keep supplies constrained, risking further price hikes at cafes and supermarkets. Prices of arabica, the variety favored by chains such as Starbucks Corp. for specialty brews, climbed as much as 4.1% to an all-time high of $4.3795 a pound.”

U.S. Economic Bubble Watch:

October 21 – New York Times (Lydia DePillis): “The economic effect of past government shutdowns has been straightforward. The economy loses some activity for a few weeks, then gains it back after the government reopens. The net cost is basically zero. This time, the math may not be so benign. As Washington’s stalemate continues into its fourth week with no end in sight, it’s looking like this could become one of America’s longest funding lapses. During the previous record-holder, a 34-day closure in 2018, Congress passed enough appropriations bills to keep more of the government funded. This time, none have been passed.”

October 20 – New York Times (Sydney Ember): “More Americans are struggling to make their monthly car-loan payments, a sign that lower-income consumers are under growing financial pressure. The share of subprime auto loans that were 60 days or more past due reached a high of nearly 6.5% in January and has lingered near that level, according to Fitch Ratings. Repossessions have swelled, more drivers are trading in vehicles that are worth less than they owe and lenders such as CarMax and Ally Financial have warned investors about auto loan performance.”

October 22 – Axios (Neil Irwin): “Many of the world’s best and brightest want to work in the United States. But U.S. immigration policy does a bad job of enabling them to do so — undermining the nation’s economic growth, a paper… argues. Top engineering, research, and entrepreneurial talent generate outsize economic gains. But the structure of visa programs that allow new workers in isn’t optimized to select for that talent, researchers argue, so potential gains in productivity and growth are lost. There is ‘a grave mismatch between the scale of America’s national ambitions for technological leadership and the way it governs high-skilled immigration, its single most reliable comparative advantage,’ wrote Jeremy Neufeld in a paper from the Aspen Economic Strategy Group.”

China Watch:

October 23 – Wall Street Journal (Yoko Kubota): “China will seek to become more self-sufficient technologically over the coming five years, the ruling Communist Party said in a new economic blueprint, signaling no respite in its intensifying rivalry with Washington. In an outline of the country’s next five-year plan, which will guide the world’s second-largest economy through the rest of the decade, Communist leaders drew a connection between the complex challenges the country faces and their drive to fortify the country through increased investments in technology and advanced manufacturing. The blueprint signals how competition between Beijing and Washington is only likely to escalate, with China looking to further reduce its reliance on the U.S. while advancing its own technological ambitions.”

October 20 – Bloomberg: “China’s factories kept the country on track to reach this year’s growth target, powered by an export boom that’s papering over deeper vulnerabilities as leaders meet to chart the nation’s next half-decade. The world’s second-largest economy grew 4.8% in the third quarter compared with a year earlier, slightly above estimates. That laid a ‘solid foundation’ for achieving the full-year expansion goal of around 5%...”

October 19 – Bloomberg: “China’s home prices fell more steeply in September, despite recent easing measures introduced by major cities to revive the struggling property market. New-home prices in 70 cities, excluding state-subsidized housing, dropped 0.41% from August, the biggest decline in 11 months... Resale home values fell 0.64%, the steepest slide in a year… ‘The property market remains in the doldrums,’ Kelvin Lam, senior China economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics, wrote... ‘A recovery in prices is unlikely until 2027, when inventories fall to more reasonable levels.’ Used-home prices fell in all 70 major cities monitored for the first time in a year.”

October 23 – Bloomberg: “After President Xi Jinping ousted a group of top generals whose careers overlapped for decades, state media accused them of ‘severely undermining’ the Communist Party’s highest echelons of authority. China sacked nine senior military officials from the ruling party on Oct. 17 in a high-profile purge that removed several commanders and a member of the 24-man Politburo. The disgraced generals had challenged ‘the system where the CMC chairman bears ultimate responsibility,’ the People’s Liberation Army Daily wrote in a front-page editorial, referring to Xi.”

October 22 – Bloomberg: “Chinese banks helped clients offload overseas currencies at the fastest pace since 2020 last month… Lenders sold a net $51.8 billion of foreign exchange on behalf of their customers in September, the largest monthly amount since December 2020… The clients include exporters, importers and some investors in overseas financial assets.”

France/Europe Watch:

October 24 – Bloomberg (Alexander Weber): “Business activity in the euro area unexpectedly reached its highest level since May 2024 as outperformance by Germany helped offset weakness in France. The Composite Purchasing Managers’ Index compiled by S&P Global increased to 52.2 in October from 51.2 in September…”

October 24 – Bloomberg (Alexander Weber): “German private-sector activity unexpectedly jumped to its highest level since May 2023, putting Europe’s biggest economy on a firm footing at the start of the fourth quarter. S&P Global’s Composite Purchasing Managers’ Index rose to 53.8 from 52… The services sector was behind the surprise, with its index coming in at 54.5.”

Japan Watch:

October 21 – Bloomberg (Sakura Murakami and Alastair Gale): “Sanae Takaichi, the pro-stimulus conservative who has become Japan’s first female prime minister, is an energetic nationalist with a soft spot for the hard-nosed politics of Iron Lady Margaret Thatcher and the heavy metal music of Iron Maiden. Takaichi broke the glass ceiling to become the nation’s leader after a parliamentary vote on Tuesday. Her choice as leader is essentially a bet by the ruling Liberal Democratic Party that a swing back to the right will attract the younger voters who have flocked to smaller populist outfits, including the arch-conservative Sanseito party.”

October 21 – Bloomberg (Yoshiaki Nohara and Erica Yokoyama): “Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi has ordered a fresh package of economic measures aimed at easing the burden of inflation on households and companies. Takaichi… did not specify the size of the package or indicate whether additional bond issuance would be needed to finance it. A supplementary budget will be compiled to finance the measures, according to an order from Takaichi to compile the package. Details will follow, but the order suggests that the package will include subsidies for electricity and gas charges during the winter, along with regional grants to ease price pressures. It also encourages small and medium-sized businesses to raise wages and increase capital investment.”

October 23 – Wall Street Journal (Megumi Fujikawa): “Japan’s new finance minister doesn’t see the need to comment on the direction of central bank policy, she said…, noting Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda’s commitment to supporting the economy by keeping monetary conditions accommodative. ‘Gov. Ueda himself has said that it’s important to maintain an accommodative financial environment and support economic activity,’ Satsuki Katayama said… Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s preference for loose monetary policy has led many economists and investors to think that the bar for interest-rate hikes is now higher… She echoed Takaichi’s view that it is necessary for the administration and the BOJ to maintain close contact as monetary policy must be consistent with government policy.”

October 19 – Reuters (Leika Kihara): “Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda likely left Washington last week with little conviction that global headwinds will allow the central bank to raise interest rates as soon as this month, with finance officials from around the world warning of downside risks… But the resilience of the global economy… also gives him cover to proceed with a near-term rate hike if a hawkish-leaning BOJ board prefers to move sooner rather than later. Ueda, as a result, left his options open by offering few clues on the timing of a rate hike, which markets bet will happen by January of next year.”

October 23 – Reuters (Leika Kihara): “Japan’s stock market is showing early signs of overheating, the central bank said…, warning of the risk that uncertainty over U.S. trade policy could cause sharp corrections and hit financial institutions. The Nikkei stock index closed at a record high on Tuesday after Sanae Takaichi, a supporter of fiscal stimulus, clinched a parliamentary vote to become the nation’s first female prime minister. The index has surged nearly 24% so far this year.”

October 23 – Bloomberg (Toru Fujioka): “Japan’s key price measure picked up pace on higher energy costs, keeping the Bank of Japan on track for further rate hikes and underscoring a key challenge facing new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi. Consumer prices excluding fresh food rose 2.9% from a year earlier in September, up from 2.7% in August and accelerating for the first time in four months…”

October 20 – Wall Street Journal (Megumi Fujikawa): “Bank of Japan policy board member Hajime Takata called for an interest-rate increase, saying that tariff-related concerns have eased and that the central bank’s inflation goal is nearly met. ‘I have come to believe that it is vital to address the situation focusing on the level of headline inflation, which has already remained at 2% and above for the past three and a half years’’ Takata said... ‘I believe that now is a prime opportunity to raise the policy interest rate.’”

October 21 – Associated Press (Yuri Kageyama): “Japan’s exports grew 4.2% in September…, on robust shipments to Asia that offset a decline in exports to the U.S., which were impacted by President Donald Trump’s tariffs. Japan’s exports to Asia jumped 9.2% last month compared to the same period a year earlier… Exports to the U.S. dropped 13.3%, marking the sixth straight month of on-year declines, while those to China surged 5.8% compared to last year. Auto shipments to the U.S. dropped 24.2% in September.”

Emerging Market Watch:

October 20 – Financial Times (Joseph Cotterill, Michael Pooler and Beatriz Langella): “A series of blow-ups in Brazil’s corporate bond market has pushed up dollar borrowing costs across Latin America’s biggest economy, drawing parallels with the jitters in US credit following the collapse of auto parts group First Brands. Bonds issued by waste manager Ambipar, petrochemicals behemoth Braskem and biofuels maker Raízen have slumped in price over the past month, sending a chill through Brazil’s corporate credit market. While investors say all three moves have been driven by issues specific to the companies, the speed and timing of the sell-offs reflect nervousness in global credit markets following the failures at First Brands and subprime auto lender Tricolor. The US backdrop had reminded investors that ‘accidents can happen anywhere in credit markets’, said Jeff Grills, head of US cross markets and emerging markets debt at Aegon Asset Management.”

October 22 – Bloomberg (Martha Viotti Beck and Giovanna Bellotti Azevedo): “Brazil’s government sees the recent spate of blowups in the nation’s corporate credit markets as isolated cases that do not pose a systemic risk, people familiar… said. The economic team is monitoring a series of setbacks that sent Brazilian corporate bonds spiraling over the past few weeks, the people said…”

October 23 – Bloomberg (Vinicius Andrade and Kelsey Butler): “Mexico has become the top borrower among developing countries in a standout year for emerging market bond sales, as it ramps up efforts to prop up indebted state oil company Petroleos Mexicanos. The country has raised just over $41 billion in hard-currency sovereign bonds so far this year — a record for Mexico and more than double the amount issued by Saudi Arabia, the next most-prolific borrower…”

Levered Speculation Watch:

October 23 – Wall Street Journal (Jack Pitcher): “Risky leveraged exchange-traded funds are booming. Their performance over the past year highlights their pitfalls. The funds, accessible to anyone through most brokerage accounts, use derivative contracts or borrowed money to amplify the return of an underlying asset… Those single-stock funds have grown rapidly in number, and gained a following among individual investors who yearn for even bigger returns than what a record-setting stock-market rally has offered. By mid-October, leveraged single-stock ETFs had accumulated some $40 billion in assets… Leveraged ETFs typically collect about 1% on the assets they manage, well above the 0.3% fee averaged by active funds. Roughly 200 leveraged equity ETFs have been launched in 2025 alone, bringing the total number to 701 funds…”

October 22 – Financial Times (George Steer, Costas Mourselas and Amelia Pollard): “Traders betting against runaway US stocks are blaming indiscriminate retail investors for their worst year of returns in half a decade. A basket of the 250 US stocks most popular with short sellers has surged 57% this year, hurting the traders betting on those shares’ decline, according to calculations by data group S3 Partners. That marks the best run for heavily shorted US stocks since 2020, when the basket gained 85%.”

October 24 – Bloomberg (Annika Inampudi): “Jane Street, Citadel Securities and other nonbank firms now command a fifth of global trading revenue, with their market share poised to hit 30% by the end of the decade as they eat into banks’ business, according to… Boston Consulting Group. Growth in trading among so-called nonbank financial institutions, or NBFIs, has surged over the past two decades. Their market share is up from just 1.6% in 2010…”

Social, Political, Environmental, Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

October 20 – Axios (Emily Peck): “Amazon Web Services, the biggest cloud computing provider, went down Monday morning — crippling thousands of services from some of the biggest companies on earth. For all its complexity and size, the global economy is fragile — breaking just one weak link drives big disruptions, online and in the real world. Amazon's East Coast region, responsible for a lot of the internet, was the culprit. Zoom, Venmo, WhatsApp and many gaming, banking, social media and consumer sites saw large spikes in reported outages, according to tracker Downdetector. The site said more than 11 million people worldwide reported issues with more than 2,500 companies… — and this was the biggest AWS outage of the year.”

October 22 – Bloomberg (Lauren Rosenthal): “The US saw more than $101 billion in losses from severe storms and fires in the first half of 2025, setting a record. That’s according to a database of billion-dollar disasters that a nonprofit has re-launched since the Trump administration formally abandoned work on it in May. In a new analysis…, scientists tallied damage from severe weather events through June, which has become the costliest such period in 45 years of records.”

October 21 – New York Times (Sachi Kitajima Mulkey): “The world is not moving quickly enough to achieve global goals to reduce methane emissions by the end of the decade, a United Nations report found. Four years ago, over 100 countries signed a pledge to cut global methane emissions by 30% by 2030. The greenhouse gas is released by the burning of fossil fuels, leaky landfills and industrial agriculture. It warms the planet up to 80 times more than carbon dioxide over two decades and is responsible for roughly a third of all planetary warming.”

October 22 – Wall Street Journal (Leandro Narloch and Carlos Poggio): “The American left and right look increasingly like their Latin American counterparts. Democratic mayoral nominee Zohran Mamdani wants New York City to open five municipally owned grocery stores to lower food prices. The plan may sound modern and progressive, but to anyone from Latin America, it sounds eerily familiar… On the right, meanwhile, President Trump’s brand of protectionism mirrors Latin American trade policies. When the White House imposes tariffs and vows to shield American industry from foreign competition, Brazilians must feel a sense of déjà vu. This is the same ‘import substitution’ strategy that Brazilian governments have embraced for the past 60 years…”

Geopolitical Watch:

October 20 – Financial Times (Andy Bounds): “The Netherlands has curtailed intelligence co-operation with the US over fears the Trump administration could use it to violate human rights or assist Russia. The heads of civilian and military intelligence said in a joint interview with Dutch newspaper de Volkskrant that they had become more cautious in what they share given the ‘politicisation of our intelligence’. It is the frankest admission by a foreign spy agency about the consequences of US President Donald Trump’s interference in intelligence matters.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Waiting For Growth To Return: SentinelOne's Inflection Still Elusive
data.description:
Despite being down ~33% in the past year, SentinelOne (NYSE:S) is still not a buy for me. I like the Purple AI monetization trends quite a lot. There are encouraging signals in terms of platform consolidation, cash flow inflection, gross margin expansion, and operating leverage.

However, I remain on the sidelines waiting for cues on sustained revenue growth potential. I am concerned about SentinelOne's competitive moat - the cybersecurity TAM is not supportive enough if the competitive edge is not maintained against strong competition in the segment.

Competitive Position

When trying to assess the competitive position for SentinelOne, dated IDC reports and several other market research reports indicate variations in how market share is measured. The market share appears to be hovering around the 4% mark, but whether it is growing or not is difficult to gauge going by third party reports alone.

I independently tried to gauge how SentinelOne's competitive position is shaping up, and the fundamental indicators are not very supportive. After a global outage that should have pressured CrowdStrike's retention metrics and opened up outflow toward competition, I still saw high retention rates in CrowdStrike (~97%) in Q4 FY2025. More importantly, there was hardly much impact seen on SentinelOne's topline despite the opportunity to ramp up acquisitions after a key and large competitor stumbled. There was a minor upward move in Jan 2025, but overall no sign of widespread migration into SentinelOne.

The slowdown in revenue growth so early in the cycle with low single digit penetration is definitely a worrying indicator. CrowdStrike, with a far higher market share (and revenue of 4-5x) is still growing at a similar pace as SentinelOne (~21% YoY increase in revenue in Q2 FY2026). Extrapolating that trend does not indicate any competitive growth for SentinelOne - it will do well to retain its position and grow along with neutral TAM expansions at best. While the market is indeed large and there is room for several players, SentinelOne's valuations should acknowledge the smaller market it is likely to cater to going ahead as well - unless growth returns.

Growth is further challenged as competition is heating up in the consolidated platform space - SentinelOne's USP. Microsoft's Defender for Endpoint is a genuine threat to the platform advantage SentinelOne plays on. The bundling advantages with Microsoft may mean many mid-market organizations already in Microsoft's network will not be willing to switch. Palo Alto has also invested heavily in a multi-product platform approach (Strata/Prisma/Cortex).

Overall, I am concerned about the future of point products in the face of larger players growing further in providing multi-product deals ranging from firewalls to XDRs and identity. To remain relevant, SentinelOne must expand into adjacent telco data signals to match telemetry breadth and maintain near zero reliability incidents to protect its AI automation and detection quality moats. Larger players with scale advantages and cross-signal platform offerings could outdo SentinelOne, or at least keep its growth checked. Any macro weakening could, in fact, drag growth even below 20%, and that could be a multiple contraction trigger.

Limited Improvements Elsewhere

The revenue growth deceleration so early has impacts on margins and profitability outlook. While cloud migration and AI efficiencies help margins, there is not much room to account for competitive or macro pressures on pricing or increases in customer acquisition costs in newer modules.

A slowing growth also pushes back GAAP profitability timelines. Although operating margins have improved, GAAP profitability is still looking like a matter of 3-4 years at least, even if execution continues to excel. Even the non-GAAP income margin, reported as ~5% in Q2 FY2026, has less room for expansion, if topline growth slows down. In a dropping revenue growth environment, driving operating leverage upward due to scale efficiencies is only going to be slower.

On a standalone basis, I find the progress toward better operating leverage and margins quite impressive. It again boils down to the decelerating growth at an earlier than expected phase of growth that is hampering margin prospects. Which is exactly what the Rule of 40 usually helps evaluate. For SentinelOne, a free cash flow margin just about positive (~1-2%), while the growth is in the early twenties, falls way short of the 40 threshold - and effectively highlights my concerns of an untimely deceleration and insufficient pace of growth.

Valuations Do Not Reflect The Risks

The EV to forward revenues ratio of ~5x may look cheap compared to, say, a CrowdStrike (~25x) and also its own recent history, but it is still not a reason to enter when the company is decelerating this far away from GAAP profitability (~-30% net income margin).

A comparison with Rapid7 shows how the rule of 40 makes a huge difference in valuations. Rapid7 has a revenue and net income scale similar to SentinelOne's and is valued at ~2x forward EV/Sales. The premium valuations due to a better TAM in endpoint (vs. vulnerability management, as in the case for Rapid7) are understood, but Rapid7's lower valuations set the bar for profitability-growth profiles. For a more diverse and mature cybersecurity player like Palo Alto too, the forward EV to sales ratio is ~14x, with a profitable market leader position (7-8x revenue scale) and still growing in the early teens.

Since the growth estimates peg ~20% topline growth in the next 2-3 years, even if those expectations are met, I do not expect much upside in share prices, as valuations will not rerate from here. I would consider buying SentinelOne only if there is a pullback to $15 that increases the margin of safety. More importantly, fundamental cues like re-acceleration and guidance beats in the upcoming quarter are more logical and better supported entry points for fresh positions. Any Purple AI revenue growth or traction detail (measurable and quantified) in the upcoming quarters could also be a good entry trigger. For now, SentinelOne remains a strong Hold at best. The valuations should not tempt investors into fresh buying yet, as competitive pressures are real, and even maintaining current growth rates does not seem to imply any asymmetric reward in the near term.

data.title: Winnebago Industries: Bullish Outlook Is Still Winning With Robust Fundamentals
data.description:
Three months after my previous analysis, Winnebago Industries, Inc. (NYSE:WGO) has already risen by over 20% and justified my buy rating. As a relatively new investor here, buying when it hit $28-29 was strategic on my part, as it further highlighted its undervaluation. At this point, I believe it’s important to reassess the stock, especially since it just released its Q4 2025 release. Despite the price increase, I believe that WGO still has some upside potential that justifies its valuation. Technicals also present bullish signals. But watch out for the recent overbuying that may prompt profit-takers to act on it.

WGO Q4 2025: A Solid Ending

The RV market still faces some uncertainty as macroeconomic conditions remain volatile. Yet, Winnebago Industries, Inc. once again proved its resilience during the fourth quarter of FY 2025.

Its operating revenue amounted to $777.3M, up by 7.8% YoY from $720.9. This was also the first time it realized positive growth after many quarters. But in my last coverage, we have already seen some improvements, as its performance appeared more stable than in previous quarters.

Its solid revenue growth after its weakness for a long time was primarily driven by its motorhomes and boats. This should not be surprising since we’ve already seen some improvements in market shipments since June. If you remember, Motorhome shipments rose by 22.4% YoY in the same month and exceeded demand growth in Towables. This continued in July as Motorhomes saw a 15.3% YoY increase in shipments. I think one key driver was the shift in consumer preferences. In most of my articles that covered RV stocks, I repeatedly mentioned the reemerging preference for RVs amid stubborn inflation. An important consideration is their lower travel spending, so I believe road trips have become more popular. In turn, RV benefited from it and became a substitute for hotels. Another factor was the weaker oil and fuel prices that made road trips less expensive.

Considering the trend, WGO took advantage of it by setting strategic prices for motorhomes. As you can see, the average price of motorhomes improved by 3.8% as volume rose by 12.9%. So, revenue also rose by 17.2%. The same goes for its marine segment since WGO took advantage of the hot demand for its boats that also led to high revenue growth. Meanwhile, towable demand in the market weakened. To manage this without sacrificing margins amid stubborn inflation, WGO only increased the average price by less than 1%. Overall, I believe that its product mix and pricing strategies are well-positioned.

More interestingly, WGO remained efficient with its improved cost and expense management. I think this was correlated to its prudent product mix that helped it improve inventory management. With that, it also generated positive income.

Possible Risks

Stubborn inflation is still a primary challenge for the RV market, and even established companies like WGO have to deal with it. As of the most recent report, inflation is starting to pick up its momentum again after climbing to 2.9%. This is the third time inflation has increased after it dropped to its 12-month low at 2.3%. This still puts cost pressures on production across industries, and I bet RVs are no exception. Purchasing power and discretionary spending are also affected. That was why many Americans downsized their summer travel spending.

WGO must also watch out for the impact of new tariffs, especially on materials and parts it imports. Retaliation may also take place, which can hurt its export costs. The consolation is that WGO may have already anticipated it. If we look at its inventory level from Q1 to Q3 2025, the sudden increase may have been due to inventory restocking to delay the impact of new tariffs. That way, WGO may keep more time to adjust to potential price surges. And hopefully, inflation will stabilize by 2026, so it can stabilize revenues and margins.

Drivers And Risk Mitigants

1. RVs Are Still Popular: If there’s one good thing that came out of a tighter travel budget, it’s that people are starting to appreciate road trips and RVs again. Increased RV purchases or rentals may be expected as inflation remains stubborn. WGO may expect resilient demand not only from personal use but also for smaller businesses that rent out RVs.

That is why it should not surprise us that motorhome shipments rose by 11.3% YoY last August. For towables, I think the decrease was not mainly due to their weakening appeal. Rather, it may be due to the fact that there have been a lot of towable shipments and purchases in the past year. The thing is, WGO can still afford to increase RV and boat prices as demand remains solid overall.

In addition, over 7.4M Americans planned to travel in RVs over Labor Day Weekend last month. And I believe the trend will not stop there because 46% of RVers may travel more this autumn. In fact, 60% of Americans noted the affordability of RV travel, so they will take an RV trip this fall. This is supported by economic uncertainty, so the switch from hotels to RVs is possible. It is worth noting that Millennials and Gen Zers have the largest YoY increase in RV purchase interest. So, its popularity continues to younger generations.

2. Interest Rate Cuts May Boost Borrowing Confidence And RV Purchases: The 25-bps interest rate cut last month could be another driving factor for RV demand and sales. This could boost borrowing confidence, including RV loans. After all, lower interest rates mean lower borrowing costs. In addition, lower interest rates may bolster economic activity. It can also improve labor market conditions and wages to help more people and smaller businesses afford RVs.

3. Weaker Oil Prices May Have Some Positive Spillovers: Oil prices are still weaker on a YoY basis despite the sudden price uptick this week. Brent is at $65.7 per barrel, while WTI Crude is now $61.5 per barrel. Despite this, I think their prices will remain at sub-$70 levels. If not, I expect them to stay lower than in 2024. After all, their price increases have already priced in geopolitical tensions and economic sanctions. This can be offset by the strategic output hike by OPEC. For this month, OPEC increased its supply by 137,000 barrels per day on top of the 548,000-bpd hike last August.

With lower oil prices, fuel expenses may also remain low. This makes road trips cheaper than in the previous year. This can entice more RV trips this holiday season and boost demand for new/used RVs and RV rentals.

4. Its Fundamentals Are Robust: Considering its solid topline performance, WGO was able to enjoy positive cash inflows. In fact, it had an operating cash flow of $181.4M, which could cover about 18x its capex. This also led to an FCF ratio of 22%, which means that WGO converted that portion of sales into free cash using the FCF/Sales Ratio. And if we look at the trend in the past 11 quarters, operating cash flow has remained enough to cover capex. FCF is also enough to cover debt repayments and dividends.

WGO complements it with prudent debt management. As you can see, no borrowings will mature within this year. It is in line with its net debt/EBITDA of 3.0x, which means that WGO only needs three years to pay the whole amount at once even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it has an adequate buffer to sustain its operating capacity and even dividends.

Valuation

After the Q4 2025 earnings release, I decided to update its valuation using price ratios. Using P/B, WGO only trades at 0.90x its book value per share, which can be considered quite cheap. If we compare it to its five-year average of 1.45x, the stock appears relatively underpriced. If we use this to find the fair value, the TP will be $62.87. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $56.58. The same applies to P/S since the stock only trades at 0.40x its sales per share versus the average of 0.51x. Using this, the TP will be $51.03. Either way, the stock is still cheap today.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. I started with the annualized dividends of $1.40 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.35. To get its dividend growth rate, I computed the 2022-2025 growth rate so as not to include the impact of the pandemic. Lastly, I got its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 6% instead of the standard 5% to price in more risks. I also got its Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function on the daily price change of the stock versus the S&P 500. Considering all these numbers, I estimated a TP of $58.33. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $52.50.

Lastly, I used the EV model based on its revenue guidance for FY 2026. The estimated revenue high and low will be $2.95B and $2.75B, respectively. Meanwhile, its EV is the reported amount of $1.54B on Seeking Alpha. So, the EV/revenue high and low will be 0.52x and 0.56x. This gives a midpoint of 0.54x. More interestingly, the reported EV/Revenue FWD is 0.54x. Considering this, we can say that WGO has already priced in the revenue guidance at its current level. This also proves that the stock trades at a reasonable level.

Technicals

The stock is still close to its one-year low but shows early bullish signals. After the Q4 2025 release, buyers flocked to the stock, which pushed the price up in a single day. It is also worth noting that the price crossed above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, suggesting a potential bullish breakout. In addition, the 50-day SMA has been increasing since August. This means that market sentiments have been improving with a positive outlook.

Its MACD and RSI can explain the trend. Note that the MACD line has already crossed above the signal line, which shows that buying volume has increased substantially in the past few days. It is also now within positive territory along with the histogram, which means that buying volume has already outpaced selling volume. RSI verifies this as it shows a surge in momentum. Yet, it advises some caution since the stock appears to be overbought. There may be some selling pressures or dips due to profit-taking.

Conclusion

Winnebago Industries, Inc. remains a solid RV player with its prudent product mix and pricing strategies. Its strategic inventory management and robust liquidity position it well against headwinds and ensure high operational sustainability and dividend longevity. It also stays reasonably cheap even after the sharp price jump yesterday. Technicals adhere to these aspects as momentum shows a potential bullish breakout, although a dip due to profit-taking is still possible. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: US IPO Weekly Recap: 5 Small IPOs Debut, As Government Shutdown Drags On
data.description:
Five small IPOs debuted and four SPACs listed this week as the government shutdown continues to complicate listings. Three IPOs and five SPACs submitted initial filings.

Singapore-based crypto mining equipment maker Bgin Blockchain (BGIN) priced its downsized US IPO at the midpoint of the range to raise $30 million at a $679 million market cap. Bgin is a digital asset technology company with proprietary cryptocurrency mining technologies, focused on alternative cryptocurrencies. The company designs and sells mining machines equipped with Bgin's proprietary 8nm or 12nm ASIC chips under different series dedicated to the mining of KAS coins, ALPH coins, and RXD coins. Bgin finished the week down 22%.

Chinese chemicals distributor Texxon Holding (NPT) priced its downsized US IPO at the top of the range to raise $10 million at a $110 million market cap. Texxon offers product search and matching, recommendations, order management and payments, and delivery logistics for plastic and chemical products. Texxon finished the week down 2%.

Taiwan-based whiskey distributor Agencia Comercial Spirits (AGCC) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $7 million at a $136 million market cap. Agencia offers imported whiskies to its clients, with its primary services including bottle whiskey sales, raw cask whiskey sales, and cask-to-bottle and distribution. Agencia finished the week up 12%.

Dubai-based travel agency Ambitions Enterprise Management (AHMA) priced its US IPO at the low end of the range to raise $6 million at a $118 million market cap. Ambitions operates as a tour operator, travel agency, and provider of event planning and management services, providing MICE (meetings, incentives, conferences, and exhibitions) management and one-stop tourism services. Ambitions Enterprise Management finished the week up 12%.

Hong Kong wellness center operator Charming Medical (MCTA) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $6 million at a $68 million market cap. Charming Medical provides Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM)-inspired therapies and products through its four wellness centers in Hong Kong. Charming Medical ended the week up 85%.

Four SPACs also listed. Harvard Ave Acquisition (HAVAU), led by executives from PE firm Plutus Partners in South Korea, raised $145 million. France-based Lafayette Acquisition (LAFAU) raised $100 million. Calisa Acquisition (ALISU) raised $60 million, targeting businesses in Asia. Growth-focused Miluna Acquisition (MMXTU) also raised $60 million.

data.title: indie Semiconductor's Pivot To Higher Margins: The emotion3D Acquisition
data.description:
The recent acquisition of Vienna-based AI perception software developer emotion3d is a pivotal moment in the lifecycle of indie Semiconductor, Inc. (NASDAQ:INDI). Until now, INDI was a pureplay developer of hardware (with related minor software) for automotive semiconductor systems. This was a momentum story given the strong growth potential of the automotive automation market; however, so far, INDI was only focusing on half the story. Doubtless, at ~$149 billion TAM, automation hardware was a big market; but that left out the ~$90 billion TAM automation software market for INDI, while its larger rivals diversified broadly. With this acquisition, INDI is now poised to quickly enter this higher-margin growth area.

Overview of indie

indie is a fabless designer of automotive semiconductor systems like ADAS or Advanced Driver Assistance Systems. The company broadly described itself as “developing advanced silicon, photonics, and sensor system solutions demanded by the automotive, industrial mobility, and adjacent applications.” The company’s broad portfolio of radar + vision + photonics + in-cabin electronics lets them access a total addressable market or TAM of $149 billion. This is a high-growth market with ~10+% CAGR opportunities as momentum grows in the automation market. As the company notes, theirs is the “pursuit of the uncrashable vehicle.”

In the next 5 years, INDI estimates a 6x growth in their solutions adding to vehicle silicone content:

They expect to ramp up SoC shipments to most of the major automobile companies of the world to the tune of 500+ million in 2025, up from 400 million last year. As of last year, their strategic design-win backlog was a staggering $7.1 billion, 72% of which was from ADAS.

The company’s recent purchase of emotion3D with its AI perception software suite lets them immediately create an accretive and high margin revenue stream through licensing royalties. INDI can bundle their vision and radar SoCs with emotion3d’s in-cabin (driver/occupant monitoring) and external sensing (camera-radar fusion, night vision) to create a synergistic software-hardware ensemble. First, software is a high-margin business, second, per McKinsey, automotive software will become a $83 billion market by 2030 (and $135 billion for automotive semiconductors ), and third, this is INDI’s first real foray into automotive software - so the acquisition takes on a lot of importance for the future of the company.

The emotion3d acquisition: rationale and synergies

indie has a track record of strategic growth through acquisition. For example, between 2021 and 2023, indie acquired companies like TeraXion (2021, $159 million) and EXALOS (2023, $65 million) for their LiDAR and photonics components. These two acquisitions positioned it to offer complete photonic sub-systems for LiDAR and heads-up displays.

The August 2025 acquisition of emotion3D comes with modest deal terms - $20 million in upfront cash and another $10 million performance contingencies. Compared to the two earlier buys, this one is small, but it could be transformative for the company, signaling its first foray into automotive software. While small, emotion3D has multiple tier 1 and OEM automotive clients, and has been described as a “leader” in the perception software space.

This acquisition is a major step towards making indie a full stack offer, complementing its silicon with emotion3D’s AI-driven software for camera/radar data interpretation. Such a strategy is similar to what Mobileye (MBLY) and Qualcomm (QCOM), indie’s larger rivals, adopt, combining silicon with perception software. Unlike pure hardware sales (~50% margins are standard), software can be both higher margin (~90% margins are not uncommon) and recurring or license-oriented, which will improve indie’s gross margins.

I have already mentioned the much larger TAM that will open up for indie through this acquisition. I should note that the company tells us that “ADAS and automated driving software will comprise over 50 percent of this total, marking a significant incremental revenue opportunity for automotive semiconductors.” This helps it gain new customers as well as open up new revenue streams from existing customers who prefer a one-stop type shop for these services.

Emotion3D already has experience partnering with major companies, like Ambarella (AMBA) and onsemi. With Ambarella – a leading vision SoC supplier – emotion3D collaborated to optimize driver and occupant monitoring software on Ambarella’s CV series processors. With onsemi, a top image sensor maker, Emotion3D worked on reference designs for DMS/OMS cameras. Also, as showcased at CES 2023, Emotion3D co-developed an intelligent restraint system with Tier-1 Veoneer. These varied collaborations highlight the hardware-agnostic approach of Emotion3D, which will now be accretive to indie. There will also be opportunities to cross-sell; so indie can sell its chips to Emotion3D’s customers, as well as bundle Emotion3D software to its own chip customers.

Emotion3D’s software does both in-cabin monitoring and exterior ADAS sensing. The in-cabin part monitors driver facial features, eye gaze, and occupant status in real time via cameras and can detect if the driver is becoming drowsy or distracted and whether occupants have seat belts on and so on. The software algorithm conforms to advanced regulations like EU General Safety Regulation, Euro NCAP protocols etc. In the exterior sensing department, the software can offer enhanced nighttime vision and camera-radar sensing fusion. These tasks can be handled in a low-compute environment suitable for automotive-grade processors. Other ways to reduce costs include the Emotion3D team’s expertise in synthetic data generation and simulation to train AI models. This reduces the cost of real-world data generation, which is a costly component of training.

Market Expansion and New Opportunities Post-Acquisition

emotion3D’s capabilities immediately help expand indie’s market into newer areas. One of these is DMS/OMS, or Driver/Occupant Monitoring Systems. indie’s hardware (camera + IR + sensors) can be directly integrated with emotion3D’s software to provide a complete in-cabin monitoring solution to vehicle manufacturers. New regulations like the European GSR open up these markets by regulating that new vehicles sold in the EU must include drowsiness and distraction detection systems. According to GSR, all new vehicles sold in the EU must have Driver Drowsiness and Attention Warning (MID-2024), and by 2026, an Advanced Driver Distraction Warning (camera-based) system will be mandatory in all new cars.

These European regulations are widely emulated in other countries. As emotion3D tells us:

Therefore, it is safe to say that the protocols and roadmaps of Euro NCAP carry importance for the global automotive scene.

As technology continues to advance and these systems become more refined, we can expect even greater improvements in road safety. emotion3D is at the forefront of innovation when it comes to all things in-cabin analysis. Our technology covers all current regulations and NCAPs

In the US as well, NHTSA is studying ways to reduce driver distraction, and various US automakers are actively implementing these DMS systems. This is fast expanding the TAM for OMS/DMS, and companies that specialize in this field - Seeing Machines (OTCPK:SEEMF), Smart Eye (OTCPK:SMTEF) - are seeing traction. With the acquisition of emotion3D, indie can now directly tap into this market, with the added advantage of offering both hardware and software.

Outside the cabin, emotion3D has algorithms for external ADAS perception that can create opportunities for indie in forward collision avoidance, pedestrian detection, and sensor fusion systems. emotion3D’s camera-radar sensor fusion is a case in point. It combines camera vision with radar and LiDAR data for better object detection, tools that are highly useful for advanced ADAS and semiautonomous driving. Indie, which already produces radar sensor chips and LiDAR components, can now offer the perception software to fuse those sensors’ data. This sort of vertical integration improves indie’s competitiveness, making it more attractive to Tier 1 manufacturers looking for complete solutions. In areas like low-light pedestrian detection too - part of emotion3D’s nighttime vision solutions - the indie+emotion3D combo can offer vertically integrated products. As the industry gradually moves toward more automated driving (over a 5+ year horizon), Indie’s combined hardware/software offering could capture a slice of that market, competing (on a smaller scale) with players like Mobileye’s SuperVision or Qualcomm’s Arriver platform in specific niches (e.g. radar-centric ADAS). All of this goes towards increasing value per vehicle and cross-selling opportunities.

Competitive Landscape – How Indie Stacks Up vs. Larger Peers

Indie used to be a hardware-only player, but this new acquisition now pits it directly against larger, vertically integrated players. There are opportunities for both competition and partnership, given some of the larger players like Ambarella were already collaborating with emotion3D for its software.

Ambarella at around $3.65 billion market cap is a mid-sized chip manufacturer. Starting out as a video processing chipmaker for cameras and drones, it now offers computer vision SoCs for automotive industries worldwide. Ambarella’s CVflow family of AI processors targets ADAS cameras, and the company, like Indie, has augmented its silicon with software acquisitions (e.g. the purchase of Oculii for radar perception algorithms in 2021). Ambarella also partners with independent software makers - like with emotion3D itself - to offer DMS solutions on top of its chips. Ambarella’s revenue ($285 million in 2024) is higher than indie’s ($217 million in 2024), but valuation-wise, Ambarella at 10x EV/revenue is less attractive than indie at 5x. Both are unprofitable growth plays, and both are trying to improve margins through vertical integration. Since Ambarella started a few years early, its gross margins (58-60%) are still higher than indie’s 50%. Historically, AMBA was better at high-performance imaging chips, while indie’s strength was mixed-signal integration and now photonic sensors. Given Ambarella’s focus on camera-based systems, Indie’s inclusion of radar, LiDAR, and ultrasonic domains could allow it to differentiate via a more holistic sensor suite, basically punching above its weight and attracting automotive manufacturers who often look for multiple sources of supply.

Mobileye is the behemoth in automotive vision/ADAS, with 80% of the market share in Level 1/2 ADAS vision systems and 2024 revenue of $1.65 billion. MBLY offers the largely adopted EyeQ system-on-chips along with its full software stack (for lane keeping, automatic emergency braking, adaptive cruise, etc.) for a complete ADAS solution. While much larger, Mobileye has more capabilities in camera vision and front-facing ADAS (camera-based), while indie has capabilities in multi sensor components and algorithms for in-cabin and other applications. Manufacturers generally use Mobileye for front-facing ADAS and another vendor for driver monitoring, opening an opportunity for the likes of indie.

Valuation-wise, MBLY has adjusted gross margins of ~67%, reflecting its software-heavy focus. It trades at around 6-7x sales, which is strong for such a large company. indie should replicate Mobileye’s playbook, improving margins as it focuses more on software using the emotion3D acquisition, offering more vertically integrated products in areas where Mobileye is not a major player.

Larger players like Qualcomm and Nvidia (NVDA) have entered the automotive sector with powerful SoCs. Some of them offer full stack capabilities, with Qualcomm acquiring Arriver’s ADAS software from Veoneer in 2022. These players offer a co-option scenario for the likes of indie; for example, an OEM using Qualcomm’s platform might still need Indie’s LiDAR lasers or its ultrasonic chips. indie can do well in a cost-sensitive, specialized situation where going with larger players may not make sense.

Large OEMs like Bosch and Denso often have their own software products. Here, indie’s smaller size may actually offer advantages in the form of better customization, lower costs and flexibility. There are also pure software plays like Seeing Machines (Australia) and Smart Eye (Sweden), two leading DMS algo suppliers. The competition is, thus, intense, but indie has advantages at multiple levels, which it can harness to capture larger market shares, both horizontally and vertically.

Financials, Valuation, and Outlook

Current financial snapshot and development highlights: In Q2 2025, indie had $51.6 million in revenue, down 1.4% Y/Y but above guidance midpoint ($51 M – $52 M); driven by radar and vision programs entering early production. Non-GAAP Gross Margin of 49.1% was stable quarter over quarter. It is expected to reach 49 – 50% in Q3 as product mix improves with ADAS ramps. Following the 2024 restructuring, non-GAAP OpEx has reached $39.9 million, and the company expects this to go down further with $8-10 million in quarterly savings. Total cash, including restricted cash, was $202.9 million. Cash burn fell sharply from $25 million in Q1 to $10 million in Q2, and is expected to stabilize at around $7.5 million on average over the next two quarters, helping the company achieve breakeven OpEx. The company repurchased $30 million worth of 2027 convertibles at a discount, saving on interest and improving EPS. They are also exploring a sale or IPO of their 34% stake in WuXi indie in order to free up cash and focus on ADAS.

Other developments include their flagship 77 GHz radar chipset, which is nearing production with Tier 1 customers. Their Vision portfolio (iND880 ISP/processor) is also gaining traction with Chinese OEMs for camera and occupancy monitoring systems, with plans for design-to-production in < 12 months.

Valuation relative to peers: indie’s EV/sales is much lower than its larger rivals like Ambarella and Mobileye. This discount reflects execution risks and the company’s early stage. Looking at it another way, there is also upside potential if the company can execute properly. Even a move to 6x sales (still below Ambarella) at, say, $300 M revenue in a few years would imply an ~$1.8 B EV, over double today’s level.

If we look at the strategic backlog, as well, that is nearly 6x its market cap as of today. While a design win is not guaranteed revenue, it does indicate the value of the company’s programs. If some of those convert to actual sales, I see a lot of upside potential.

Risks and Challenges

While I like the long-term outlook based on the emotion3D acquisition, integration is an execution challenge investors should be well aware of. Management has to allot bandwidth for retaining talent, engaging existing customers and acquiring new ones, all the while retaining bandwidth on its ADAS hardware vertical.

The acquisition also opens indie up to greater and more intense competition from larger players well-entrenched with software+hardware products. These larger companies have advantages of scale that indie will be competing against. Moreover, its closed stack nature may not always appeal to customers comfortable with sourcing hardware and software from different vendors.

In the short to midterm, tariffs may have an impact on indie’s business. Although the company assures us that it has geographical bundling in manufacturing and customers, so that tariff impact is minimal, this is not always true in certain markets. With the tariff war intensifying, the situation remains fluid and uncertain.

Investment Summary and Conclusion

indie offers a differentiated investment opportunity in a growing niche, and its early-stage nature implies there is still considerable upside. This new software acquisition takes it to an inflection point from where there is a good chance that it will grow further, seeing higher margins and improved earnings. The bull-case rests on execution, so if you are confident in management’s ability to do so, this is a buy at dips.
